title: Charmed Life - Chapter 12 (Fragment 4)
text:
She raised the wood up and showed Cat the signs painted on the red-coated back of the glass itself.

“Cabala, I think,” said Cat. “It’s a spell.”

“So she did think of it!” said Janet. “Really, it’s hell having a double. You both get the same ideas. And working on that principle,” she said, sliding Gwendolen’s letter between the plywood and the glass and pressing the plywood back in place, “I bet I know what the spell’s for. It’s so Gwendolen can have a look from time to time and see how Dear Replacement’s getting on. I hope she’s looking now.” Janet swung the mirror back to its usual position and crossed her eyes at it, hideously. She took hold of the corners of her crossed eyes and pulled them long and Chinese, and stuck her tongue out as far as it would go. Then she pushed her nose up with one finger and twisted her mouth right around to one cheek. Cat could not help laughing. “Can’t Gwendolen do this?” Janet said out of the side of her face.

“No.” Cat giggled.

That was the moment when Euphemia opened the door. Janet jumped violently. She was much more nervous than Cat had realized. “I’ll thank you to stop pulling faces,” said Euphemia, “and get out of your nightdress, Gwendolen.” She came into the room to make sure that Gwendolen did. She gave a croaking sort of shriek. Then she melted into a brown lump.

Janet’s hands went over her mouth. She and Cat stared in horror as the brown lump that had been Euphemia grew smaller and smaller. When it was about three inches high, it stopped shrinking and put out large webbed feet. On these webbed feet, it crawled forward and stared at them reproachfully out of protruding yellowish eyes near the top of its head.

“Oh dear!” said Cat. It seemed that Gwendolen’s last act had been to turn Euphemia into a frog.

Janet burst into tears. Cat was surprised. She had seemed so self-assured. Sobbing heavily, Janet knelt down and tenderly picked up the brown, crawling Euphemia. “You poor girl!” she wept. “I know just how you feel. Cat, what are we to do? How do you turn people back?”

“I don’t know,” Cat said soberly. He was suddenly burdened with huge responsibilities. Janet, in spite of the confident way she talked, clearly needed looking after. Euphemia clearly needed it even more. If it had not been for Chrestomanci, Cat would have raced off to get Mr. Saunders to help that moment. But he suddenly realized that if Chrestomanci ever found out what Gwendolen had done this time, the most terrible things would happen. Cat was quite sure of this. He discovered that he was terrified of Chrestomanci. He had been terrified of him all along, without realizing it. He knew he would have to keep both Janet and Euphemia a secret somehow.

Feeling desperate, Cat raced to the bathroom, found a damp towel, and brought it to Janet. “Put her down on this. She’ll need to be wet. I’ll ask Roger and Julia to turn her back. I’ll tell them you won’t. And for goodness sake don’t tell anyone you aren’t Gwendolen—please!”

vocabulary.definition: Loaded with a heavy responsibility or stress.
vocabulary.example: Cat feels overwhelmed by the situation.

vocabulary.definition: A mystical Jewish tradition or secret knowledge, often associated with magic or spells.
vocabulary.example: Identifies the mystical means behind the spell on the glass.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or showing certainty about something.
vocabulary.example: Expresses Janet’s outward certainty.

vocabulary.definition: Making deep, hoarse sounds similar to a frog or raven.
vocabulary.example: Describes Euphemia’s voice or noise before transformation.

vocabulary.definition: The condition of eyes looking inwards towards the nose, either intentionally or unintentionally.
vocabulary.example: Describes Janet’s deliberate eye movement for a funny face.

vocabulary.definition: Slightly wet or moist.
vocabulary.example: Describes the towel Cat brought for Euphemia.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or showing hopelessness and urgency.
vocabulary.example: Shows Cat’s urgent emotional state to help.

vocabulary.definition: In an extremely ugly or disgusting manner.
vocabulary.example: Describes the exaggerated and grotesque facial expression.

vocabulary.definition: An intense feeling of fear, shock, or disgust.
vocabulary.example: Expresses the reaction to Euphemia’s transformation.

vocabulary.definition: To move suddenly and forcefully due to surprise or fear.
vocabulary.example: Describes Janet’s startled reaction to Euphemia's appearance.

vocabulary.definition: A compact mass of a substance, usually solid but shapeless.
vocabulary.example: A description of Euphemia’s frog form as an indistinct mass.

vocabulary.definition: To change from solid to liquid or figuratively to disappear gradually.
vocabulary.example: Describes Euphemia transforming physically into something else.

vocabulary.definition: Easily agitated or anxious; apprehensive.
vocabulary.example: Expresses Janet’s anxious emotional state.

vocabulary.definition: A type of strong wooden board made from thin layers of wood glued together.
vocabulary.example: Material used in constructing or hiding the letter behind the glass.

vocabulary.definition: A fundamental truth or proposition that serves as the foundation for a system of belief or behaviour.
vocabulary.example: Used here to explain reasoning based on a fundamental idea.

vocabulary.definition: Sticking out or projecting beyond a surface or boundary.
vocabulary.example: Describes the visible eyes of the frog creature.

vocabulary.definition: To feel or show great joy or delight.
vocabulary.example: (Not in the text, so omitted)

vocabulary.definition: In a way that expresses blame or disapproval.
vocabulary.example: The frog’s gaze conveys disapproval toward the humans.

vocabulary.definition: Something kept hidden or unknown to others.
vocabulary.example: Refers to concealment of their condition.

vocabulary.definition: Confident in one’s own abilities or character.
vocabulary.example: Describes Janet’s usual confident demeanor before crying.

vocabulary.definition: Crying noisily while taking in deep breaths.
vocabulary.example: Describes Janet’s emotional reaction.

vocabulary.definition: In a serious, thoughtful, or solemn manner.
vocabulary.example: Shows Cat’s serious attitude towards the problem.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
vocabulary.example: Refers to the magical inscription or charm seen on the glass.

vocabulary.definition: In a gentle and caring manner.
vocabulary.example: Describes Janet’s compassion towards Euphemia.

translations.ru: обратить обратно, вернуть в прежнее состояние

vocabulary.definition: To restore someone or something to a previous state or condition.
vocabulary.example: Refers to reversing Euphemia’s transformation.

vocabulary.definition: Feet with skin between the toes, typical of frogs and aquatic animals.
vocabulary.example: Physical description of the frog form’s feet.
